lines: Oren kneels by the intake barricade, fitting split boards back into a shape that might hold.
"The cult pulled the nails clean, like they had all night and no fear of interruption. I keep asking what made them so sure no soldier would come."

choices.label: Ask how the chapel held out

lines: Oren grips a rusted pry bar with both hands.
"I kept the chapel fence. Useful work, if nobody writes a song about hinges. A soldier needs soup, a dry sock, and someone plain enough to say the wall is still standing."
"Lately no one came to lean on that fence and complain. I keep planing the thought and finding the same knot."
"The Hallowfen men used to come through with mud on their knees and lies in their mouths."
"They complained about Runa's stew, drank Nessa's water, slept under our roof, then went back to pretending this place was only a supply mark on a ledger."
"Then nothing: no boots, no bell, no runner with bad news. Bad news at least has the manners to arrive."
"Aye. Men run; bells do not. Road horns do not get scared and crawl under a bed."
"Something stopped the road before the knives found us. I keep turning that nail over with my thumb."
"Some do. Most come back for their socks."
"Laugh if you like, but a man who abandons a post still remembers dry feet. Nobody came for the socks."
"Fence, hinges, shutter slats, little repairs the Remnant never writes songs about."
"A post will betray you slower than a bishop, but it gets there. You watch both for cracks."
"It will not stop a determined saint or a man with a proper axe."
"It might stop a fool with a knife. Most bad nights bring at least one fool, so I build for him."
Oren looks down at the pry bar as if surprised to find it still in his hands.
"I keep meaning to set it down. Then I remember how many doors in this house used to lock from the outside."
